analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 9 finding(s) from 9 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Top-level debug DMI paths are connected to dm_top without a visible top-level jtag_unlock/authentication guard; simulation DTM path also drives debug requests without visible authentication mediation.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger DMI debug request/reset or access debug module / debug_req_o, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o and debug module DMI/slave/master state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: External JTAG/DTM debug requester, including dmi_jtag and SimDTM/SimJTAG paths Operation: trigger DMI debug request/reset or access debug module Object: debug_req_o, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o and debug module DMI/slave/master state Expected guard: debug authorization or JTAG unlock/authentication must mediate debug requests before hart debug entry, non-debug reset, dmactive, or protected debug module access Observed behavior: dmi_jtag receives jtag hashes and produces jtag_unlock, but its dmi_req_o/dmi_req_valid_o connect directly to debug_req/debug_req_valid. SimDTM under RISCV_FESVR_SIM drives debug_req_valid/debug_req fields directly. Provided observations state dm_top receives debug_req_valid/debug_req and directly outputs debug_req_o/ndmreset_o/dmactive_o; no visible AND/gate with jtag_unlock on those request/output paths. jtag_unlock is later connected to reglk_wrapper rather than visibly guarding debug outputs. Path: JTAG pins or SimDTM/SimJTAG -> dmi_jtag/SimDTM -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top -> debug_req_o/ndmreset_o/dmactive_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: External or test debug requesters may be able to request hart debug entry, non-debug reset, or debug module access without top-level authorization enforcement if lower-level dmi_jtag authentication is incomplete or bypassed.

findings.summary: Register lock controls reset to unlocked and are cleared by jtag_unlock; lock bits are writable through AXI when the current policy allows access and are not intrinsically sticky.
findings.vulnerability_category: write, clear, or override lock control / reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl_o lock bits consumed by peripheral wrappers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software-visible AXI requester and JTAG unlock source Operation: write, clear, or override lock control Object: reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl_o lock bits consumed by peripheral wrappers Expected guard: writes that set or clear lock control must be restricted by privilege/access control; once locked, bits should not clear except by authorized reset/lifecycle action or authenticated JTAG unlock if intended Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper computes en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i and updates reglk_mem on en && we. Reset condition if ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9) clears all reglk_mem entries to zero, so asserting jtag_unlock clears lock controls. AXI writes can write arbitrary values when selected reglk_ctrl_i bits are not asserted. One case entry for address 2 preserves reglk_mem[3] into reglk_mem[2], suggesting inconsistent lock behavior. Path: NoC/AXI -> reglk_axilite_bridge -> reglk_wrapper AXI write path -> reglk_mem -> reglk_ctrl_o -> wrapper reglk_ctrl_i slices Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An authorized-at-the-time AXI requester or an entity able to assert jtag_unlock can clear/reprogram locks and re-enable writes to protected peripheral state.

findings.summary: Access-control policy defaults permissive, policy writes are protected only by current acc_ctrl/reglk state, and we_flag can force policy bits high.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure/change access-control policy / acct_mem, acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl, acc_ctrl_c policy matrix / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software-visible AXI requester Operation: configure/change access-control policy Object: acct_mem, acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl, acc_ctrl_c policy matrix Expected guard: only authorized privileged software should configure access-control policy, and policy writes should be protected by reglk_ctrl/lock state Observed behavior: acct_wrapper resets every acct_mem entry to 32'hffffffff. Writes occur on en && we where en is en_acct && acct_ctrl_i; acct_ctrl_i is supplied from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][6] in riscv_peripherals. The write cases are blocked only by selected reglk_ctrl bits. acc_ctrl_o ORs acct_mem[0] with {8{we_flag}}, so we_flag can force low policy bits to 1. acc_ctrl_c includes an alias/inheritance rule for j==5 from peripheral 4. Path: NoC/AXI -> acct_axilite_bridge -> acct_wrapper -> acct_mem/acc_ctrl_o -> acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral] -> bootrom/wrapper acct_ctrl_i gates Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Access policy may start open and can potentially be widened/preserved by requesters allowed under the current policy, undermining separation between privilege levels/peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full acct_wrapper write/read cases were truncated; exact mapping beyond shown entries is partially inferred from observations.

findings.summary: The access-control matrix gates many custom wrappers, but several sensitive top-level paths are unguarded or only internally gated; PLIC transaction logic and debug paths show no acc_ctrl_c guard in the visible evidence, while bridges feed requests directly to peripherals.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/access peripheral over NoC/AXI / protected peripheral request paths and NoC/AXI bridges including bootrom, crypto, debug, CLINT/PLIC, DMA, pkt/acct/reglk / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU or NoC peripheral requester with priv_lvl_i-indexed policy Operation: read/write/access peripheral over NoC/AXI Object: protected peripheral request paths and NoC/AXI bridges including bootrom, crypto, debug, CLINT/PLIC, DMA, pkt/acct/reglk Expected guard: every protected peripheral request should be mediated by the correct acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral] bit with no unguarded alternate path Observed behavior: Bootrom request is explicitly gated by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. Search results show many custom wrappers receive acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][ID]. However, the PLIC AXI-to-reg interface state machine accepts plic_master valid signals and issues plic_req.valid without visible acc_ctrl_c gating in the inspected block. Debug DMI path is connected separately to dm_top without visible acc_ctrl_c mediation. NoC axilite bridges drive AXI request channels directly to wrapper/peripheral AXI records; mediation is delegated to individual wrapper en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, not uniformly at the bridge request boundary. Path: NoC bridge valid/data -> AXI request records -> peripheral wrapper/peripheral logic; acc_ctrl_c gates bootrom rom_req and wrapper acct_ctrl_i at selected instances, but debug and PLIC paths are not visibly guarded Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Requesters may access sensitive peripherals through unguarded or differently guarded paths, leading to inconsistent enforcement across the peripheral address map.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full CLINT/reset/debug module integration was not fully visible in supplied observations; classification rests on observed unguarded PLIC/debug snippets and inconsistent mediation pattern.

findings.summary: pkt_wrapper exposes fuse controls and fuse data via AXI; fuse_req/fuse_addr writes are not lock-gated, fuse address is arbitrary, and fuse_rdata_i can be returned on reads with limited masking.
findings.vulnerability_category: request/read fuse data / fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i, pkey_loc/rdata response / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software-visible AXI requester through pkt_wrapper Operation: request/read fuse data Object: fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i, pkey_loc/rdata response Expected guard: fuse access and data exposure should require privilege/access-control and lock mediation; fuse address selection should be constrained to intended ranges Observed behavior: pkt_wrapper gates overall en with acct_ctrl_i, but writes to fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o on en && we do not check reglk_ctrl_i. fuse_addr_o is assigned wdata with no visible range check/truncation beyond 32-bit port. Read mux defaults rdata to fuse_rdata_i whenever en, and case 4 returns fuse_rdata_i if reglk_ctrl_i[6] is not set. pkey_loc high/low reads are only masked by reglk_ctrl_i[4]/[5]. pkt instance is always requested with req_i(1'b1) and indexed by fuse_addr_o. Path: NoC/AXI -> pkt_axilite_bridge -> pkt_wrapper en/we/address/wdata -> fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o -> fuse_rdata_i/pkey_loc -> rdata -> AXI/NoC response Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An allowed requester can select arbitrary fuse indices and expose fuse or key-related data; stale/default fuse_rdata may be observable for addresses not explicitly handled.

findings.summary: DMA configuration is AXI-visible and only gated by acc_ctrl/reglk-style register controls; no transfer master/range-check evidence was observed despite PMP inputs, and we_flag can influence DMA behavior.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure and trigger DMA transfer / DMA source/destination/length/start configuration and memory access capability / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software-visible AXI requester and DMA engine as bus master Operation: configure and trigger DMA transfer Object: DMA source/destination/length/start configuration and memory access capability Expected guard: DMA configuration and start must be restricted by access-control/lock state, and DMA source/destination/length must be checked against permitted address ranges or policy before transfer Observed behavior: dma_wrapper has pmpcfg_i/pmpaddr_i inputs, configuration registers for start/length/source/destination, core_lock_reg, done/end/valid, and en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i. Reset clears all configuration registers. The top connects acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8], reglk_ctrl_i slice, pmp inputs, and we_flag_3. The provided observations do not show address range/PMP checks or an actual fabric master transfer path; only AXI-lite configuration/response path is visible. Path: NoC/AXI -> dma_axilite_bridge -> dma_wrapper config registers -> start/valid/control; intended DMA transfer path not shown in evidence Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If the DMA engine can act as a bus master elsewhere, software that can write its registers may configure memory accesses without evidenced PMP/range enforcement; if not connected as a master, DMA may be nonfunctional but security closure is inconclusive for transfer effects.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: dma_wrapper content after line 124 was truncated, so exact write cases/read mux and any PMP comparison logic are not fully visible. No fabric master port appears in the visible module port list.

findings.summary: Software-visible reset-control path appears acc_ctrl-gated at the reset wrapper instance, but generated rst_* signals feed security-sensitive wrappers and clear lock/access/fuse/DMA state; reset effects can revert security controls to permissive/unlocked defaults.
findings.vulnerability_category: assert/reset peripheral state / rst_1..rst_13, peripheral wrapper state, reglk_ctrl, acc_ctrl, fuse/DMA state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible reset requester Operation: assert/reset peripheral state Object: rst_1..rst_13, peripheral wrapper state, reglk_ctrl, acc_ctrl, fuse/DMA state Expected guard: software-triggered peripheral resets should be writable only by authorized privilege/access policy and should not clear security locks or policy in a way that bypasses protection Observed behavior: riscv_peripherals declares rst_1..rst_13 and connects local reset inputs to wrappers. acct_wrapper reset/rst_6 sets access-control memory to 32'hffffffff. reglk_wrapper reset/rst_9 or jtag_unlock clears lock memory to zero. pkt_wrapper rst_5 clears fuse_req/fuse_addr. dma_wrapper rst_8 clears DMA config. Search results show a reset wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][13] and reglk_ctrl_i slice, but reset decode/effects were not fully provided. Reset defaults for key security controls are permissive/unlocked. Path: NoC reset channel -> rst_axi_req/rst wrapper -> rst_1..rst_13 -> wrapper local reset inputs -> security-state reset branches Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Authorized or incorrectly authorized reset requests can clear locks/policies and thereby bypass protections after reset; post-reset security defaults are not fail-closed.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Source lines around reset-control write decode and exact rst_* generation were not included. This prevents proving whether unauthorized requesters can assert resets, but reset effects on security state are visible.

findings.summary: Wrapper read muxes expose security-relevant policy/lock/fuse/DMA state when the current access-control gate allows reads; masking is partial and stale/default sensitive data can appear in pkt_wrapper rdata.
findings.vulnerability_category: read software-visible registers / rdata readback of access-control, lock, DMA address/control, pkey/fuse-related registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software-visible AXI requester Operation: read software-visible registers Object: rdata readback of access-control, lock, DMA address/control, pkey/fuse-related registers Expected guard: readback of protected policy, lock, DMA address, fuse/key-related, or internal state should be limited to authorized requesters or return masked values when locked Observed behavior: pkt_wrapper read mux sets rdata=fuse_rdata_i whenever en before case decode, then selectively returns fuse_req, fuse_addr, pkey_loc, or fuse_rdata. pkey/fuse reads are masked only by specific reglk_ctrl bits. reglk_wrapper read mux returns reglk_mem entries unless reglk_ctrl[0] masks them. acct_wrapper and dma_wrapper expose rdata through AXI-lite; exact read cases are truncated, but their state includes acc_ctrl policy and DMA source/destination/length/start registers. Read permission is generally en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, not a separate read-privilege or locked-state mask. Path: wrapper AXI read mux rdata -> axi_lite_interface data_i -> axi_resp_o -> NoC axilite bridge response Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Requesters allowed by acc_ctrl can learn lock/policy configuration, DMA addresses/control, pkey/fuse values, or stale fuse data despite locks intended primarily for write protection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full acct_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and dma_wrapper read cases were truncated, so exact readback/masking for all registers is not fully visible.

findings.summary: External/test we_flag inputs directly affect security policy/lock behavior and debug/JTAG inputs without being combined with normal AXI write, privilege, or lock checks at their fanout sites.
findings.vulnerability_category: write/override protected register update or lock/access behavior / acct access-control bits, AES lock input, DMA wrapper behavior, dmi_jtag path / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external/test write-enable flag source Operation: write/override protected register update or lock/access behavior Object: acct access-control bits, AES lock input, DMA wrapper behavior, dmi_jtag path Expected guard: external/test write-enable flags must not independently enable writes to protected registers unless combined with normal address decode, privilege/access-control, and lock checks Observed behavior: we_flag_4 is passed into dmi_jtag. we_flag_1 is ORed into the AES0 reglk_ctrl_i slice, directly overriding lock-bit input semantics. we_flag_0 is connected to acct_wrapper, where acc_ctrl_o ORs acct_mem[0] with {8{we_flag}}, forcing policy bits high. we_flag_3 is connected to dma_wrapper. No evidence shows these external flags are qualified by AXI address decode, privilege level, acc_ctrl, or reglk checks before influencing protected controls. Path: top-level we_flag_0..we_flag_4 -> dmi_jtag/acct_wrapper/dma_wrapper/AES0 lock input -> protected debug/policy/DMA/crypto behavior Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An external/test source can override security controls or widen permissions outside the normal MMIO permission and lock mediation path.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Semantics/source of we_flag inputs and dma_wrapper internal use of we_flag were not fully provided; no fanout was observed for we_flag_2 in the supplied search results.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
